If the architect is never brought the boundary to enforce, or assumes someone downstream made it real the way a human used to fill that gap out of habit, the product manager believes the line exists because they specified it, and it does not exist at all. Each person did their job. The system failed in the hand-off neither believed was theirs.

The worked example: choosing the wall

Take a concrete case: an agent with authority to modify records in a production system, one action among several that would be expensive to reverse. The product manager has already named the boundary correctly: this category of action requires a human to approve it first. What is left is how that boundary gets made real, and it is the architect's to answer, not the product manager's and not the engineer's by default.

The procedural version is documented, reviewed, signed off, and lives in the system prompt the agent reads before it acts. It is cheap and easy to change, and it works right up until an unusual combination of inputs convinces the agent that this case is the exception the instruction did not anticipate.

The physical version, on a modern enterprise platform, looks like scoping the agent's identity so the destructive action is not an operation its credentials can invoke at all, and gating the call in the platform layer itself, upstream of the agent's reasoning, the way platform-level guardrails on a cloud environment enforce access independent of what any calling application requests. The agent can want the action, argue for it, even believe it is right, and still not take it, because the capability was never wired into what it holds. It costs more to build and is harder to change later. It is also the only version that holds on the day the agent decides the rule does not apply, and choosing it is what belongs at the architect's desk, not with whoever happens to be provisioning credentials that week.

What the triad never seated

Go back to the three-seat team. It had a decider, a shaper, and a builder, and no seat whose job was to say a decision is not automatically true just because someone wrote it down. That gap did not matter when a human engineer sat between the decision and the running system, filling it with judgment out of habit, catching the mismatch before it shipped. It matters now, because the thing filling that loop is fast, confident, and will not catch its own exception.

The architect is where that judgment has always lived, and the agentic product is the first kind of product that makes the absence of her seat impossible to hide. She does not own the decision of where the line goes. She owns whether the line is real, and she carries something the triad never had a chair for: the standing veto over a boundary that looks finished on paper and is not finished at all. The team that skipped her seat to move faster has not removed a cost. It has removed the only check on the difference between what it decided and what actually holds, and it will not find out which one it built until the day the agent tests it.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Green Checkmark Nobody Owned
entry.content:
The Green Checkmark Nobody Owned

Post 9, The Agentic AI Team series

An enterprise agent handles vendor onboarding. It checks a new supplier against policy, flags exceptions, and clears the routine cases without a human in the loop. Before launch it ran through the full evaluation suite: hundreds of test cases, every known edge case, every answer the team agreed a correct agent must give. Green across the board. The team shipped.

Eleven weeks in, someone on the compliance side notices the agent is clearing vendors against a sanctions-screening threshold the company tightened two years earlier. The agent's answers are confident, well formatted, consistent with every example it was trained on. The evaluation suite tested whether the agent's output matched the reference data it was built against. It did. The reference data encoded the old threshold. The suite checked exactly what it was built to check, and passed, and what it proved had quietly stopped being what the release meeting thought it proved.

This is a post about one green checkmark and how many people have to have done their job for it to mean what everyone in that release meeting assumes it means.

What a passing eval actually certifies

Start with what an eval is, because the rest depends on being precise here. An eval runs the agent against a golden dataset: a curated set of inputs paired with the outputs a team has agreed to accept, the edge cases it knows about, and the answers the agent must never give. A green result means the agent's behavior matched that dataset. Nothing more.

The assumption nobody states out loud is that the checkmark is only as good as the dataset behind it, and the dataset is a human artifact that can be wrong, incomplete, or simply out of date. The vendor-onboarding agent passed because the golden dataset encoded the old threshold as correct. The suite did not malfunction. It faithfully certified the agent against a reference that had expired, and faithfully reported green, because grading the agent against the dataset is exactly what a suite does with whatever it is handed.

A pass rate compounds this in a second way. A single green run against a non-deterministic agent is a sample, not a proof. Run the same case again and the agent, drawing on different retrieval and context, may answer differently. The honest measure is the pass rate across many runs and the shape of the worst slice, not one clean pass that happened to land well. And a suite that checks each step of a multi-step process in isolation has tested the steps, not the system: ten steps each ninety-five percent reliable compound to something closer to sixty percent reliable end to end, because the rates multiply rather than average.

None of this shows up in a release meeting. What shows up is a color.

Four owners behind one color

Lay the failure next to the people who could have caught it, and a structure appears that a simple two-party story, engineering runs the tests and the PM decides what ships, does not have room for.

Engineering owns the harness. It builds the infrastructure that executes the suite and reports the result, and on the vendor-onboarding team it did that correctly. That work has not changed and it is real.

The eval owner sits between engineering and the product manager and owns whether the number can be trusted at all. That means curating the golden dataset and keeping it current as the policy it encodes changes. It means running each case enough times to report the worst outcome, not the lucky one. It means calibrating the automated judge, the model that scores the agent's answers at volume, against a set of outputs a human has already labeled, because judges have documented biases toward longer answers, toward whichever answer appears first, toward their own model family, and an uncalibrated judge has an error rate nobody has measured. It means writing down which scenarios were tested and which were knowingly left out. It means treating a vendor's silent model update as a deployment event that triggers a fresh regression run instead of an invisible change nobody re-checked.

The domain expert owns the one thing none of the other three roles can supply: whether the answer the golden dataset calls correct is actually correct in the world right now. A product manager cannot personally know that a sanctions threshold moved two years ago. A compliance specialist can, and only a compliance specialist can, and the agent ran for eleven weeks against a retired standard precisely because that specialist was never a standing owner of the dataset that trained and graded it.

The product manager owns the gate: what success means, what an acceptable failure looks like, which tradeoffs among cost, speed, and reliability the product will accept, and the decision to ship. That is still a real and necessary job. It is just no longer the whole job, because the PM is gating on a number that three other people have to stand behind for the gate to mean anything.

The seam is the checkmark itself

Trace the vendor-onboarding failure hand-off by hand-off and the shape becomes obvious.

The product manager defined success correctly: the agent must clear or flag vendors according to policy. Engineering built the harness and ran the suite faithfully, exactly as instructed, against the golden dataset it was given. That dataset encoded a threshold that someone, at some point, had captured as correct, and nobody owned keeping that capture current, so it aged silently while the dashboard stayed green the entire time. The compliance expert who could have flagged the outdated threshold may have been consulted once, at the start, but was never a standing owner of the dataset's ongoing correctness, because nobody had defined that as a role.

Every individual did their job. The checkmark still went green, and the green was a kind of lie that no single person told. It was assembled out of a correct harness, a faithful test run, a stale dataset, and a missing owner, each of which looked fine in isolation. That is what a seam failure looks like: one role produces a signal, other roles rely on it without inspecting it, and the failure lives in the hand-off nobody believed was theirs to check.

On a team that has actually closed this seam, a green eval is not finished the moment the suite passes. It is finished when the eval owner can state the pass rate across repeated runs, the judge's calibration, and the coverage gaps, and the domain expert has signed that the dataset still reflects the current rules, and only then does the product manager read the green checkmark as meaning what the release meeting needs it to mean.

What travels with the checkmark

There is one discipline that binds the four roles together, and it belongs to the eval owner to enforce: a passing result has to arrive with its provenance attached, not just its color.

A checkmark that shows up as a single green light has its chain of custody hidden, and a hidden chain of custody is exactly where a stale threshold survives eleven weeks undetected. A checkmark that arrives with its pass rate across runs, its worst slice, its judge calibration, its coverage gaps, and a domain expert's sign-off is a checkmark the product manager can actually gate a launch on, because every link in that chain has a name attached to it.

The difference between those two checkmarks is invisible in the room where the ship decision gets made. Both render as the same shade of green on the same dashboard. It becomes visible weeks later, when someone in compliance, or audit, or the field reads an output closely enough to notice what the suite never actually checked. When that happens, the question "who signed off on this" needs an answer that already exists, not one assembled after the fact under pressure.

What the PM keeps, and what moves

The product manager does not lose the gate. Deciding what success means, what tradeoffs the product accepts, and whether to ship on a given day is still squarely a PM responsibility, and nothing about a four-owner model changes that.

What moves is the assumption that the PM can personally vouch for the number behind the gate. Keeping the golden dataset honest as the world changes around it is not a PM skill, and it was never really an engineering skill either; it needed a name and an owner of its own, which is what the eval owner role is for. And knowing whether a specific answer is still correct in a specific domain, whether a threshold, a guideline, or a policy quietly moved out from under the agent, is not something evaluation engineering can manufacture no matter how well the harness is built. It has to come from someone who lives in that domain and is accountable for saying when the ground has shifted.

Take the agent your team is closest to shipping and find the one eval number everyone in the room already trusts. Ask, of specific people rather than the dashboard: who keeps the reference data current, who calibrated the judge, who signed that the answers it grades against are still correct today, and what it would look like for that number to stay green while the rule underneath it has already changed. If the answer to any of those is that no one owns it, the suite is not the problem. The gap between the roles is, and it is still open.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how a passing test result depends on ownership the traditional software team never assigned. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Employee You Never Onboarded
entry.content:
The Employee You Never Onboarded

Post 10, The Agentic AI Team series

A company stands up an agent that triages inbound support tickets, drafts responses, and escalates the hard cases to a person. Procurement logged it as a software purchase. IT logged it as an integration. Nobody in the people function heard about it, because that function is for people, and this was a subscription. Six months in, the agent handles a quarter of all ticket volume, its behavior has drifted twice after silent model updates nobody noticed, and when a customer complaint forces the question of who has been watching it, the honest answer is no one. Not because the team is careless. Because the job was never written down anywhere a person could be assigned to it.

That gap is not a bug in this rollout. It is what happens every time an organization adds an actor with its own judgment and processes the addition as a purchase instead of a hire.

An actor with judgment, and no file on it

When an organization adds a person to a team, an apparatus switches on without anyone deciding to switch it on. The person is interviewed against the role. Someone checks references, which is really someone asking whether this person's behavior under pressure matches their resume. They are onboarded: told what they own, what they may not touch, who to escalate to. They have a manager who notices when their work drifts, and they can be coached, reassigned, or let go, with a record of why. None of this feels like infrastructure, because it is old enough to be invisible. It is a century of organizational practice built to answer one question: how do you put an actor with its own judgment into a system and stay accountable for what it does.

An agent goes through none of it. There is no interview, because the procurement category was software license, not hire. There is no onboarding beyond a system prompt written in an afternoon. There is no manager, because managing it is in no one's job description. There is no termination clause, because you do not fire a subscription. The apparatus that would have caught a human's problems was never pointed at it.

Call this anthropomorphizing and the objection deserves a straight answer. The claim is not that the agent thinks the way a person thinks. The claim is that the org chart cannot tell the difference, because the apparatus exists for an actor that takes action, not one that is conscious. If a system decides inside a scope of authority and hands results to people who treat them as input to their own work, it behaves like a team member whatever is or is not happening under the hood. Call it what you actually have: a contractor with broad system access, no statement of work, and no way to end the engagement.

That is structural, not a lapse by an otherwise careful organization. An agent fits no slot the org chart already has. It is not an employee: no contract, no review cycle, no manager. It is not a vendor: no sales representative answers for its behavior, and its behavior changes without a release anyone approved. It is not a tool in the old sense, since a tool does what it is told and this one decides. It sits between the categories, and none of the governance built for any of them applies.

What onboarding, a manager, and an exit mean at the org level

That gap will not close on its own, and it takes a different shape at the org level than on a single engineering team. Two supervisory seats are the minimum answer. One is the agent supervisor, sometimes folded into an "AgentOps" function, whose job is to watch what the running agent is doing right now and notice when its behavior changes. The other is the eval owner, who keeps answering whether the agent is still correct, not just whether it is still running. Neither seat existed eighteen months ago. Both now do, at the organizational level, what a manager and a performance review already do for a human hire.

Onboarding, at this level, is a decision about scope before the agent ever runs: what it may do, what it may never touch, what happens the moment it hits a case outside that scope. It is a product and risk decision, shared by whoever owns the product and whoever owns security, and it has to exist before deployment, not get inferred from an incident afterward.

A manager, at this level, is not a person reading the agent's output line by line. It is the agent supervisor or AgentOps seat, whose actual week includes watching the fleet: how often it escalates, how often a human overrides it, whether its behavior this month resembles last month's. That watching does not stop, because the agent does not hold still, and a supervisor who checked once at launch has inspected a snapshot of an actor that kept moving after the inspection ended.

A decommission plan, at this level, answers a question most organizations cannot currently answer: if this agent had to be pulled tomorrow, who revokes its access, and who tells the people who relied on it. A human's departure has a checklist. An agent's usually does not.

None of this is the developer's daily review loop. A team that has solved code review well can still have zero of it in place for the agent handling customer refunds, a different agent with no engineer reading its output as unforgiving, testable code. The org-level apparatus has to exist on its own.

Staffing the seats by stage

Here the honest answer stops being one org chart and depends on size. A startup that separates every one of these responsibilities into its own hire drowns before it ships anything. An enterprise that lets one person hold the entire supervisory function for every agent it runs is one incident from finding out that person had no time to do the job. The organizing idea fits one sentence: merge to decide, separate to ship. Deciding what to build can concentrate in a small group. Watching what is now running in production cannot, once there is enough of it to watch.

At a startup, one senior person can reasonably hold most of the build side of an agent: what it does, how it is structured, how it ships. That concentration is correct at this size, not a shortcut. What cannot be skipped, even at five people, is a second pair of eyes on whether the agent can be trusted, separate from whoever built it, since the builder is the worst-positioned person to catch their own agent's blind spot, for the same reason code review exists at all.

At a scale-up, these responsibilities stop being part of someone's week and become someone's actual job. An agent supervisor emerges because the running fleet has grown past what anyone can watch on the side. An eval owner emerges because keeping a golden dataset current is more work than a generalist can absorb between other duties. The product lead, who used to make every call about scope and trust, now shares that territory with people who can see drift and correctness alone. This is where organizations grow well or stumble, and the difference is whether the new seat is framed as work that finally has an owner, or as authority someone took away. Naming the work, not the rank, keeps it from becoming a turf fight.

At enterprise scale, the seats become teams, and a failure shows up that smaller organizations never encounter: the hand-off between teams becomes an organizational boundary instead of a conversation down the hall. The supervisor who used to have a direct line to the product lead becomes an AgentOps function reachable now by ticket, and a ticket carries intent worse than a conversation does. The biggest risk at this scale is rarely an unstaffed seat. It is an unowned seam between two fully staffed ones.

This supervisory layer's newness shows up in compensation. Product roles focused on building AI capability now command some of the highest pay in the industry, because the market has decided the judgment of what to build is scarce, and it is right about that. Look for an established compensation band for the agent supervisor or the standing eval owner, and it is largely absent: a well-priced market for deciding what the agent should do, a thin one for watching whether it still does it. That is the empty column from earlier in this series, showing up in the salary survey instead of the org chart. Organizations that staff these seats now are buying something scarce before the market prices it. The ones that wait will pay later, at a premium, under duress.

The box that grows with the work

Picture a small team that starts, sensibly, as three people in a room: someone who owns the product decision, someone who owns the technical structure, someone who owns whether outputs can be trusted. Call it a three-in-a-box. At launch, with one agent handling one workflow, three people can hold the whole thing between them, meeting twice a week, and that is the right size for the job in front of them.

Add a second agent, then a third, spread across the business, and the box under strain looks different. It does not disappear. It becomes the seed that three or four boxes grow from, one trio per agent, each still merging build decisions the way the original did, each also owning a staffed answer to who is watching this agent today. Scale further, to an enterprise running agents across dozens of processes, and the boxes consolidate into functions: a central AgentOps team every trio feeds into, a standing evaluation function every trio draws on rather than rebuilding its own. The three-in-a-box was never the wrong shape, only the right shape for the size the organization was at the time, and the mistake is assuming the shape that worked at five people still works at five hundred.

The question with no easy exit

So take the most important agent your organization runs and ask the questions you would ask about a new hire in a consequential seat. Who onboarded it, and into what scope. Who is its manager, in the sense of someone whose actual job includes noticing when it drifts. Who would revoke its access tomorrow if it had to be pulled, and who would tell the people relying on it. For a person in that seat, every one of those questions has a name attached. For most agents already running in production, they do not, and the discomfort of asking out loud is the most accurate measure of how much of the hiring got skipped.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the organization staffs the supervisory seats an agent requires, from a single person wearing every hat at a startup to named functions at enterprise scale. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: Re: Report 6PP1-001 does not contain any data pages
entry.content:
There is an inconsistency in your question.  If you are doing a Goods Issue to a Cost Center you would use a 201 movement type.

A 281 is a GI to a Network Activity.

Please show a screenshot of your goods movement.

If you are posting the GI to a Cost Center then they will not show up on S_ALR_87013542, that is for PS Objects.

entry.title: Re: Mandatory Nature of the Account Dimension in SAC
entry.content:
Yes, you can create a model without an account dimension. In the business content for Integrated Financial Planning
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/integrated-financial-planning-an-overview/ba-p/13544197
we use a generic dimension for the G/L Account. 
There also is a blog that discusses the pros and cons here:
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/new-model-configuration-options/ba-p/13526098
Besides the points that you mention, there are other advantages for this approach: You can use the account dimension for a free cross-calculation structure or to model opening/changing/closing balances in a financial statement.
Best regards
Hartmut

entry.title: Re: Clarification on SAP Standard Approach for Parallel R...
entry.content:
Hi ,

Have you reviewed the portal guide?

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_Solution_Manager/8b923a2175be4939816f0981b73856c7/10dad8533eb2492ae10000000a423f68.html

Release management uses a predecessor – successor relationship. A successor release can be imported into the production system only after the predecessor release has been imported. This means that a successor release cycle can be shifted to the Build phase only after the Deploy phase of the predecessor release cycle is finished.

Best regards,

Leo

entry.title: Re: EKBE-BUKRS not fetching value for delivery item.
entry.content:
BUKRS in EKBE is filled only when the PO history entry has an accounting impact. For pure logistics events, it is left blank by design.

When the PO history entry originates from a transaction that creates an accounting document (e.g., invoice receipt, goods receipt with valuation), EKBE is updated with the company code because the FI document (BKPF-BUKRS) is available and linked.

For logistics-only events (like delivery creation, stock transfer, or non-valuated goods movements), no FI document is generated. In these cases, EKBE may not carry BUKRS, since SAP expects you to derive it from the PO header (EKKO-BUKRS) or plant assignment.

If the plant is assigned to a company code (T001W → T001K) and the movement type is valuated, EKBE-BUKRS is filled. If the movement type is non-valuated (e.g., 101 without valuation, or delivery without accounting impact), EKBE-BUKRS remains blank.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News in June

June was a full month. Three releases shipped back to back, the team delivered across a wide range of areas, and the German SAP user group DSAG kept the conversations grounded with honest feedback and real customer examples at their AK Business Analytics meeting in St. Leon-Rot.

Learn more in my top features video in June 2026 on YouTube 📺.

In addition, explore the latest updates and blogs in my community news blog.

 

My top features in June

Use Object Store Sources in Replication Flows

You can now use object store sources directly in replication flows. Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake Gen2, Google Cloud Storage, SFTP, HANA Data Lake Files, and Microsoft OneLake are all supported. The data lands in local table files in Datasphere. If your raw data lives in cloud storage, you no longer need a separate ingestion pipeline.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Oracle as a Source in Replication Flows

Oracle is now supported as a source in replication flows, with Initial Only load type. Full snapshot replication from Oracle to your supported targets in Datasphere. Oracle Wallet authentication is also now supported, enabling SSL and certificate-based authentication via mTLS.

➡️  More information

 

 

Use Snowflake as a Source in Replication Flows

You can now create Snowflake connections and use them as a source in replication flows, also with Initial Only load type. For non-public Snowflake environments, Cloud Connector is supported as a reverse invoke proxy, routing traffic through a TLS tunnel.

➡️  More information

 

 

Cloud Connector Support for Confluent Cloud Connections

You can now configure Cloud Connector for Confluent Cloud connections. Same principle as Snowflake: Cloud Connector routes traffic through a TLS tunnel so replication flows can reach Confluent Cloud without public endpoints. Requires Cloud Connector to be set up in your landscape.

➡️  More information

 

 

SQL View Transform in Transformation Flows on File

You can now create a SQL view transform inside a transformation flow on file, using Apache Spark SQL. A full SQL editor with auto-complete, drag-and-drop source objects, real-time validation, and data preview. All of this running directly in your file space. This capability was explicitly listed as not supported until this release.

➡️  More information

 

 

Direct Delta Sharing Client Integration for Transformation Flows

Accessing delta share tables in transformation flows, from BDC Foundation Services or Databricks, no longer requires an intermediate step. The integration is now direct, with no intermediate copying, better performance, and reduced storage overhead.

 

 

CLI Extensions: Pause/Resume Replication Flows + File Space Import/Export

Two CLI improvements in one release. You can now pause and resume replication flows and individual replication objects via the command line. File space objects are also now accessible from the CLI: import and export of spaces, modeling objects, tasks, and connectivity. Fewer things that require manual UI interaction for teams running Datasphere through automation.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enhanced Capacity Unit Monitoring

The Capacity Unit Monitoring tool now has a customizable date and time range filter. No longer limited to the current month. A new Spaces tab shows consumption broken down per space, covering Premium Outbound Integration, object store usage, and total capacity unit consumption. Useful for anyone managing multi-space environments.

➡️  More information

 

 

Enable System Notifications ⭐ KP's Pick

You can now configure email notifications for replication flow and task chain completions directly in your user profile settings, for success, failure, or both. No more manual monitor checking to find out if an overnight run completed. Small feature, real impact.

➡️  More information

 

 

YOUTUBE LINK

 
 

DSAG AK Business Analytics — June Meeting in St. Leon-Rot

On June 19 I joined the DSAG AK Business Analytics working group in St. Leon-Rot for their summer session. These meetings have a specific quality that's hard to find elsewhere. The audience is technical, the questions are direct, and nobody is there to be sold to.

The agenda this time was particularly strong:

▶️  SAP Business Data Cloud roadmap including fresh SAPPHIRE announcements (Fabian Hartje)
▶️  SAP Analytics Cloud roadmap update (Matthias Kraemer)
▶️  AI in practice: how Swisscom uses AI in the SAP ecosystem (Tjarko von Lehsten, Viktor Seide)
▶️  BDC Object Store implementation with real-world examples (Stefan Härtelt)
▶️  360° BDC — Data Provisioning, SAC and Planning, Parts I and II (Maria Alejandra Uruena, Sandy Tran, Jie Deng)

Fresh SAPPHIRE updates, live demos, and real customer examples in one room. That's a good day. Thanks to AK speakers Jörg Steinhorst and Stefan Kahle for organizing, and to everyone who showed up and contributed to the discussion.

 

My favorite blogs in June

SAP Analytics Cloud is expanding live data connectivity beyond the classic live models. @david_stocker explains how BigQuery, Snowflake, and Azure SQL are already supported, with SharePoint and more cloud data lakes coming later this year. No replication, no import jobs. Just a live connection via Smart Data Access and virtual tables behind the scenes. The blog also explains how this underpins Seamless Planning. A good orientation read if you haven't looked at live data connectivity yet.
🔗 Live Data Connectivity in SAP Analytics Cloud - Overview and Direction

@AndreasForsterpublished a hands-on tutorial for vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with SAP Business Application Studio, Python, and an AI coding agent. Instead of writing the PAL/APL code yourself, you describe what you want and let the agent generate it. Jupyter notebooks and all. The example is a time-series forecast, but the setup works for any ML scenario HANA Cloud supports. He also shows how to add current documentation context to keep the generated code accurate.
🔗 Hands-on Tutorial: Vibe coding SAP HANA Cloud Machine Learning with BAS, Python, Claude and Cline

CDC-based replication flows in Datasphere don't use ODP queues at all. @anilkumarauce traces the full internal architecture step by step: changes are captured at database table level using logging tables, triggers, and a staging layer called the Resilient Data Buffer, all the way from an S/4HANA application table into Datasphere. Exactly the kind of under-the-hood knowledge that saves hours when something unexpected happens in production.
🔗 Under the Hood of ABAP CDC in SAP Datasphere Replication Flows

The most complete provisioning reference for SAP Business Data Cloud on SAP Community. @ShreyasriB built out an 8-part step-by-step series covering the full provisioning journey: from the initial BDC Cockpit setup via SAP for Me through Datasphere, SAC, Databricks, HANA Cloud, Intelligent Apps, and Snowflake. The kind of guide you want open in a second browser tab while doing the actual setup.
🔗 Provisioning of Business Data Cloud: Start with Part 1

 

More blogs about SAP BDC and Datasphere to check out 👇

  • SAP Enterprise Planning: Where AI meets strategy and execution
  • Business Data Architecture: Modeling Layers for Structured Transformations
  • Upcoming Change: Member Limit Enforcement for Data Action Parameters
  • Managing Datasphere Consumption: Gaining Visibility and Control Over Capacity Units

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic pages for SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere. Also check out the SAP Help for SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Datasphere, including for troubleshooting and analysis guides, onboarding guides, and more.

entry.title: Controlling the View: How Access Control and Business Services in SAP Cloud ALM Protect Your Monitor
entry.content:
The Problem with Open Access

 

Before Access Control was introduced, any user with a general monitoring role in SAP Cloud ALM could access all landscape objects — every service, every system, every message. For small teams managing a handful of systems, this was acceptable. For larger organisations with multiple business units, subsidiaries, or compliance requirements, it created a real data governance gap.

 

The solution is Attribute-based Access Control (ABAC) — a mechanism that ties visibility of landscape objects directly to user assignments, not just roles.
 

What Is Access Control in SAP Cloud ALM?

Access Control in SAP Cloud ALM allows administrators to restrict which **services**, **systems**, and **business services** are visible to which users.
Once activated, users will only see the landscape objects assigned to them through an **Access Control List (ACL)**. Users not assigned to any ACL will see nothing.

Important: Access Control activation is irreversible. Once switched on, it cannot be turned off. Careful preparation — including creating ACLs and assigning users before activation — is essential.
 

The Role of Business Services

A Business Service in Integration & Exception Monitoring is a a logical grouping that maps technical integration artefacts to a recognisable business context — for example, showing only specific iFlows from an SAP Integration Suite system, or specific interfaces from an AIF setup for a business transactions like Order-to-cash or Hire-to-Retire.

This makes Business Services the natural vehicle for **data-level access control**. Rather than granting a user access to an entire system, an administrator can:

1. Create a Business Service that includes only the relevant systems and interfaces
2. Apply filters (e.g., iFlow name, interface name, object type) to limit the data visible within those systems
3. Assign the Business Service to an ACL
4. Assign the user to that ACL

The result: the user sees only the monitoring data relevant to their scope — nothing more.
 

How to Set It Up

Step 1 — Assign the Access Controller Role

Designate one or more users responsible for managing ACLs. These users need the **Landscape Access Controller** role.

Step 2 — Create Business Services (if needed)

In Integration & Exception Monitoring, navigate to **Configuration → Business Services**. Create a Business Service, add the relevant systems, and define filters for the interfaces, iFlows, or object types that should be visible within it.

Step 3 — Create Access Control Lists

In Landscape Management, navigate to **Configuration → Attribute-based Access Control → Access Control List Maintenance**. Create one or more ACLs, each with:
- A name and description
- Assigned landscape objects (services, systems, and/or business services)
- Assigned users, with read or edit privileges

Step 4 — Test Before Activating

Use the **Test Access Control** feature to restrict individual users temporarily and verify they see only what is intended — before activating globally.

Step 5 — Activate Access Control

Once all ACLs are in place and tested, activate Access Control globally under Configuration → Attribute-based Access Control → Access Control Settings
 
Key Behaviors to Be Aware Of
- Business Services and systems are independent Granting a user access to a Business Service does not automatically grant access to the underlying systems. The systems must be explicitly included in an ACL if direct system-level access is needed.
- Rule-based vs. fixed restrictions ACLs support both rule-based restrictions (covering all landscape objects matching certain attributes, including new ones added in future) and fixed restrictions (covering one specific object by ID). Rule-based restrictions are the recommended approach for large or evolving landscapes.
- Super-users can be exempted Users who need unrestricted access across the entire landscape can be exempted from ACL restrictions individually, without the need for all-encompassing access control lists.
 

Why This Matters for Organizations

 

For organizations operating in regulated industries, managing multi-region landscapes, or simply enforcing the principle of least privilege, this combination of Access Control and Business Services provides:

- Data separation across teams and business units
- Operational clarity — teams see only what is relevant to them, reducing noise and the risk of acting on data outside their scope
- Scalability — rule-based ACLs automatically cover new landscape objects as the landscape grows
 

Further Reading

For a complete guide on setting up Access Control and working with Business Services, refer to the official SAP Help Portal documentation:

SAP Cloud ALM – Access Control

For a step-by-step walkthrough on creating Business Services in Integration Monitoring
 
Access Control in SAP Cloud ALM is a deliberate, one-way gate. Plan it carefully, test it thoroughly, and once it is in place, your organization's monitoring data will be exactly as visible — and no more — as it needs to be.

• Defective material identified during production/QC.
  • Create Rework Order (CO07).
  • Add rework operations and required components.
  • Release the order (CO02).
  • Issue materials (MIGO/261).
  • Perform rework and confirm (CO11N/CO15).
  • Post Goods Receipt for reworked quantity.
  • TECO  the order

entry.title: Re: SAP ABAP training advice please.
entry.content:
Hello Andy,

Thank you for your message and for sharing your background. With around 20 years of SAP experience in manufacturing, you already have a very strong functional foundation. This is especially valuable because ABAP development in an SAP environment benefits greatly from a solid understanding of business processes.

In my view, Java or Python knowledge is not a prerequisite for getting started with ABAP. A general understanding of programming logic is certainly helpful, and your experience as a CNC programmer may give you a good starting point. ABAP is its own programming language with its own concepts, and it can be learned step by step.

I would recommend starting with the ABAP fundamentals: data types, internal tables, Open SQL, modularization, debugging, and simple reports. Since your employer is currently using an ECC 6.0 system, classic ABAP knowledge is still highly relevant. Later on, you can build on this with topics such as ALV, BAPIs, BADIs, user exits, and the Enhancement Framework.

A structured beginner-level training would be a good way to start. You can find a suitable training here: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/acquiring-core-abap-skills

In addition, I would suggest doing small hands-on exercises in an SAP system as early as possible. ABAP is best learned by debugging, reading existing programs, and creating small reports yourself.

Wishing you every success as you get started with ABAP.

entry.title: Ask Your IT Landscape Anything: A Hands-On Guide to SAP LeanIX's Built-In MCP Server
entry.content:
Most enterprise architecture tools have the same problem: the data is there, but getting to it takes sometimes more time than you would like. That changes with MCP (Model Context Protocol). SAP LeanIX now ships with a built-in MCP server, which means you can connect any compatible AI assistant to your workspace and simply ask questions in plain language.

In this post I'll show you how to set it up and walk through eight real use cases which you can leverage at any stage of knowledge. Whether you're an enterprise architect, a program manager, or a business stakeholder, this post has something for you.

 

What is MCP and why does it matter for EA?

Think of MCP as a universal connector, it lets AI assistants connect to external tools and data sources in a standardised way. It was introduced by Anthropic in late 2024 and has since become the de facto standard for giving AI models access to real-world systems.

For LeanIX, this means your AI assistant can reach directly into your workspace, call the right tools, combine results from multiple sources, and give you a structured, contextual answer or even do tasks for you within your workspace. And this all from a single question.

The practical impact is that enterprise architects get faster answers, business stakeholders can ask questions without needing platform access or enterprise architecture knowledge, and everyone works from the same live data.

Looking further ahead, MCP is also the foundation for working with AI agents. While AI assistants typically respond to direct user prompts, AI agents extend this concept further. Agents can autonomously plan and execute multiple tool calls, reason over intermediate results, and iteratively work toward a broader goal. MCP provides the standardised interface that enables these interactions.

 

Setup: Three ways to get started

Before anything else, you need an API token from your LeanIX workspace. This token is required for all setup options below.

Go to your LeanIX workspace → click your profile icon (top right) → Administration → API Tokens → + New API Token. Copy the token and keep it somewhere safe because it is only shown once.

Once you have your token, choose how you want to connect:

 

Option A — LeanIX MCP Chat

The LeanIX MCP Chat is a dedicated chat interface built for exactly this purpose. You can access it here: LeanIX MCP Chat

  1. In the chat interface, click MCP-Servers in the bottom left sidebar → Add Server.
  2. Give it a name, select LeanIX as the Transport Type and open your LeanIX workspace in the browser to copy the instance name from the URL (e.g. demo-eu-10.leanix.net) and paste it into the Instance field. It will automatically show you the full MCP endpoint URL it will connect to.
  3. Then paste your API Token into the token field

 

Option B — Claude Desktop

In Claude, go to Settings → Developer → Edit Config and add the following to your claude_desktop_config.json:

{

  "mcpServers": {

    "LeanIX": {

      "type": "http",

      "url": "https://<region>.leanix.net/services/mcp-server/v1/mcp",

      "headers": {

        "Authorization": "Bearer LXT_YOUR_TOKEN”

      }

    }

  }

}

Replace <region> with the regional prefix of your LeanIX workspace URL and YOUR_TOKEN with the LXT_ token you generated.

Then save the file and restart your Claude.

 

Option C – Cursor

The recommended approach is to edit the MCP config file directly. Open (or create) ~/.cursor/mcp.json and add the following:

{

  "mcpServers": {

    "LeanIX": {

      "type": "http",

      "url": "https://<region>.leanix.net/services/mcp-server/v1/mcp",

      "headers": {

        "Authorization": "Bearer LXT_YOUR_TOKEN"

      }

    }

  }

}

Then restart your Cursor.

 

8 use cases that show what this actually looks like

Getting a quick lay of the land

New to a project or just need a fast briefing? Instead of clicking through multiple views, just ask.

"Give me an overview of our IT landscape - how many applications, initiatives and business capabilities do we have?"

In seconds, the AI called get_overview and returned a full count: 153 applications, 40 initiatives, 89 business capabilities, 965 fact sheets in total. A useful first question for anyone onboarding to a new account or preparing for a stakeholder meeting.

 

Catching end-of-life risk before it catches you

Tracking application lifecycle across a large portfolio is one of those tasks that's always urgent and never quite done. This query makes it immediate.

"Which applications in my portfolio are reaching end-of-life within the next 12 months?"

The AI combined get_applications and get_fact_sheet_details to return a prioritised list with EOL dates, hosting type, business criticality, and owner information. Notice that two tools were called automatically in sequence. You didn't need to specify how to get the answer, only what you wanted to know.

 

 

Keeping transformation programs on track

Portfolio managers and program leads often struggle to get a quick read on the status of multiple parallel initiatives. One question covers it.

"What is the current status of our transformation initiatives and which milestones are still outstanding?"

The response broke down all 40 initiatives by lifecycle status, highlighted the key programs with their completion percentages, and surfaced the single upcoming named milestone like Wave II finalized on 2026-11-01. The kind of summary that would sometimes take a reporting cycle to produce.

 

Understanding the blast radius before you act

Before decommissioning any core system, you need to know what depends on it. This is where the multi-tool capability of MCP really earns its place.

“If we were to decommission one of our core applications - which other applications depend on it and would be affected?” follow up question: "SAP ECC, extended impact please - applications, interfaces and business capabilities"

The AI chained eight separate tool calls with searching by name, pulling fact sheet details, traversing relations and produced a full impact map: the core ECC module applications (FI, CO, MM, HCM), their downstream dependencies, the business capabilities at risk across Finance, HR and Manufacturing, and a clear summary of what breaks if ECC goes dark. 

 

 

Closing data quality gaps at scale

Missing business owners are one of the most common data quality problems in any EA repository. Instead of manually hunting them down, you can use the AI to build and distribute a targeted survey.

"Create a survey for all application owners to fill in missing business owner information"

The AI identified 144 applications with no Business Owner subscription, drafted a complete survey proposal including title, target audience, duration, and four specific questions and asked for confirmation before creating anything.

 

 

Turning architecture insights into a clear next step

LeanIX generates architecture insights automatically based on patterns in your data. Getting to them quickly, and knowing which one to act on first, is where the AI adds value in this use case.

"What are our current architecture insights and which has the highest priority for our next review?"

The insight surfaced a functional overlap between Concur and TravelPerk in the travel and expense domain, with a clear rationale for prioritization: Concur reaches end-of-life in eight months, making this time-sensitive.

 

Finding what you don't know

Not every query returns a clean list of results, and that's useful too. When asked about GPL-licensed open source components across the portfolio, the AI ran five consecutive license searches and found nothing. Not because the problem doesn't exist, but because no SBOMs had been attached to the applications.

"Which applications in our portfolio use GPL-licensed open source libraries that could create legal risk?"

The response was honest about the coverage gap, explained exactly why the results were empty, and offered three concrete next steps: broaden the search to LGPL variants, drill down into specific high-risk apps, or set up automated monitoring.

 

Enforcing data quality standards

When standards slip, broken seals accumulate quietly across your portfolio. Rather than hunting them down fact sheet by fact sheet, you can ask once and get a complete, prioritized view of every application that is out of compliance.

          "Show me all applications that currently have a broken quality seal and explain what's missing"

The AI returned the complete set of 144 applications with a broken quality seal, sorted by last update, each with a direct link to the fact sheet. What stood out was the follow-up: the AI immediately offered three concrete next steps.  

 

What to take away from this

SAP LeanIX's MCP server is not a prototype or a workaround but it is a built-in capability with currently 40+ dedicated tools covering applications, initiatives, architecture decisions, tech stacks, surveys, SBOM data, and more. For business stakeholders, this means getting answers without needing platform access or Enterprise Architecture knowledge. For architects and IT teams, it means faster analysis with less manual effort. And for anyone responsible for data quality, it means the gaps in your EA repository become visible and actionable immediately.

 

Find the list of available MCP tools here:

Can actively modify data (write):

  • create_survey — create a new survey (poll) in LeanIX for a set of fact sheets.
  • create_survey_reminder — create/send a reminder for an existing survey run.
  • change_survey_status — change the status of a survey run (poll run).
  • create_architecture_decision — create an architecture decision from a template.
  • create_tech_stack — create a custom technology stack in the workspace.
  • delete_architecture_decision — permanently delete an architecture decision (irreversible).
  • update_architecture_decision — update an existing architecture decision's fields, status, or linked fact sheets.

Read-only / consume data (no modification):

  • get_fact_sheet_types — list all fact sheet types.
  • get_overview — workspace overview (counts, facets).
  • get_fact_sheet_details — fetch full details for up to 25 fact sheet IDs.
  • search_fact_sheet_by_name — find fact sheets by exact name.
  • text_to_fact_sheets — natural-language search for fact sheets (filter generation).
  • get_applications — search/filter Application fact sheets.
  • get_application_components — retrieve SBOM/library components for an application.
  • search_components_by_purl — search library components by PURL.
  • batch_search_components_by_purls — batch PURL lookups.
  • get_architecture_decisions — list architecture decisions.
  • get_architecture_templates — list architecture decision templates.
  • get_architecture_template_components — retrieve components of a template.
  • get_reports — list stored reports.
  • get_diagrams — list diagrams.
  • get_diagram_by_id — fetch a diagram by UUID.
  • get_initiatives — list initiatives with relations and lifecycle.
  • get_milestones_by_fact_sheet_ids — get initiative milestones.
  • get_insights — retrieve LeanIX architecture guidance insights.
  • search_transformations_tool — search transformations (projects/executions).
  • get_transformation_types_tool — list transformation type definitions.
  • search_users — find users in the workspace.
  • get_user_subscriptions — list fact sheets a user is subscribed to.
  • get_fact_sheet_subscriptions — list subscriptions for a fact sheet.
  • get_survey_templates — list poll/questionnaire templates.
  • get_survey_recipients — list who is involved in a survey run.
  • get_survey_results — retrieve survey results.
  • get_reminders — list reminders for a survey run.
  • search_survey_runs — list/search survey runs.
  • get_all_tech_stacks — list discovered tech stacks.
  • get_tech_stack_details — get details for a tech stack.
  • preview_tech_stack_matches — preview PURL matches for tech-stack rules.
  • get_architecture_decision_as_markdown — export an architecture decision as markdown with YAML front matter.
  • get_architecture_decision — fetch a single architecture decision by UUID.
  • count_fact_sheet_relations — count relations from a source fact sheet type, grouped by target.
  • search_components_by_license — search library components by license name (e.g. MIT, GPL, Apache).

Have questions or want to share your own use cases? Drop a comment below! I'd love to hear how others are using LeanIX with AI.

entry.title: Smarter Planning by Design: Introducing Business Configuration in SAP Revenue Growth Management
entry.content:
1. Static Assignments, Real Costs: Why Configurable Planning Structures Matter

For consumer products companies managing hundreds of promotions across dozens of retail partners, a rigid planning structure quietly erodes efficiency. A Key Account Manager handling a top-tier global retailer operates in an entirely different business reality than one managing a regional grocery chain — different promotion types, approval workflows, baseline sources, and planning scopes all demand distinct configurations. Yet in many trade promotion systems, these differences are handled through static, hard-coded assignments: each configuration component is manually mapped to a fixed object type, one by one, and updated by hand every time the business evolves.

The result is predictable: KAMs waste time filtering out irrelevant options, admins build workarounds to compensate for inflexible configurations, and governance gaps quietly accumulate. When planning structures don't reflect business reality, even well-designed promotions can underdeliver.

 

2. SAP Revenue Growth Management: Configurable by Design

SAP Revenue Growth Management is built for the complexity of CPG trade promotion — from promotion planning and funds management to actualization and P&L analytics. Central to this is a principle that separates modern SaaS platforms from legacy systems: the planning experience should be shaped by your business, not the other way around.

Business Configuration in SAP Revenue Growth Management gives IT admins and implementation consultants the tools to define precisely how KAMs plan — what promotion types are available, which planning structures apply to which accounts, and how the right configuration reaches the right KAM automatically. The result is a planning environment that is both governed and intuitive: KAMs see only what is relevant to their accounts, while admins maintain full control over standards and structure — complexity absorbed by configuration, not by the people who use it.

For more information on our current capabilities, see An Overview of SAP Revenue Growth Management. 

 

3. Business Configuration in Action: Three Foundations of a Governed, Flexible Planning Experience

3.1. Custom Promotion Types: Define the Promotion Logic Your Business Runs On

Before a KAM can plan a single promotion, the system needs to know what kinds of promotions your business runs — and how each should behave across trade spend, approval flows, and planning scope.

SAP Revenue Growth Management ships with four preset promotion types based on two dimensions: promotion category (short-term vs. long-term) and planning level (product-level vs. category-level). For organizations with more nuanced go-to-market strategies, Business Configuration goes further, enabling admins to create custom promotion types that reflect the full complexity of their commercial model. Each custom type comes pre-wired with its own set of rules — so when a KAM selects it, the right planning experience follows automatically.

 

Precise control over what KAMs can plan.

Each custom promotion type is defined along three axes — promotion category, planning scope (e.g. sell-in), and planning level — and then bundled with a specific set of trade spend profiles, status profiles, and workflow profiles. When a KAM selects a promotion type, only the trade spend options, statuses, and approval flows mapped to that type are available. Irrelevant options are filtered out entirely, reducing cognitive load and eliminating the risk of misgovernance.

A structured foundation for more complex promotion forms.

Promotion type configuration is not just about today's needs — it establishes the extensible framework for more advanced promotion mechanics. Once the configuration layer is in place, it will serve as the foundation for planned future enhancements, including:

  • Free goods — buy-X-get-Y mechanics, where the cost is in additional inventory rather than a price reduction
  • Tiered discounts — volume-based discount ladders where the rate increases as quantity thresholds are met
  • Non-promotion trade activities — listing fees, co-op advertising, and slotting allowances, which require the same planning, approval, and cost-tracking infrastructure as promotions, without being promotions themselves

Once a promotion type is activated and mapped to an account plan type, it becomes available to KAMs in exactly the right planning context — governance without friction, at every level of your planning organization.

 

3.2. Account Plan Types: Match Your Planning Structure to Your Market Reality

Promotion types define what KAMs can plan. Account plan types define the business context in which they plan — and in CPG, that context varies more than most systems are built to handle. A top-tier retailer driving 80% of your revenue warrants a fundamentally different planning setup than a regional partner: different promotion options, different baseline governance, different planning horizons.

Account plan types are configurable containers that bundle together the promotion types available for a given planning context, along with baseline management rules, status profiles, and planning scope. Admins can create a highly specific account plan type for a strategic key account driving the majority of your revenue — with a curated set of promotion types, tightly governed baseline sources, and a multi-step approval workflow — while maintaining a simpler, more generalized account plan type for smaller regional partners.

 

Tailored planning scope, down to how baselines are sourced and governed.

Each account plan type defines not only which promotion types KAMs can use, but also how baseline volumes are sourced and managed for that planning context.

  • For CPG companies with a demand planning system in place, the baseline can be synchronized directly from an external system such as SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP), ensuring baseline data stays current and aligned across systems.
  • For those who manage baselines closer to the field, KAMs can manually upload baseline volumes via spreadsheet, maintaining flexibility without requiring centralized planning infrastructure.

Baseline building blocks add a further layer of adaptability — for accounts where local market insight matters, admins can enable KAMs to make targeted adjustments on top of the sourced baseline, a governance decision made once at the account plan type level, not renegotiated for every account. For more information, see this blog on Baseline Management.

A planning structure that scales as your business does.

As your business grows — new sales areas, new retail partners, new planning periods — account plan types scale with it. Rather than creating one-off configurations for each new context, admins define a small set of reusable plan types that can be applied consistently across markets. When business requirements shift, updating one account plan type propagates the change across every plan built on it, without manual rework account by account.

When an account plan type is mapped to its promotion types, the essential planning elements land in every KAM's workspace automatically — the right baseline rules, the right promotions, the right governance, from day one.

 

3.3. Determination Rules: The Right Configuration, Delivered to the Right KAM — Automatically

Creating well-designed promotion types and account plan types is the first step. The harder problem is ensuring the right configuration reaches the right KAM at the right moment — without requiring manual selection that introduces inconsistency and human error.

Determination rules solve this. They are admin-defined business rules that, the moment a KAM begins creating an account plan, instruct SAP Revenue Growth Management to match the account plan's attributes against all active rules and automatically apply the most appropriate account plan type — along with everything mapped to it.

 

A governed hierarchy from broad to specific.

Admins can create determination rules at different levels of specificity: a generalized rule covering all sales areas and customers for a given fiscal year, or a specialized rule scoped to a specific sales organization, distribution channel, and customer subset. Priority across rules is determined entirely by the ranking defined by system admins, and a default preset rule acts as the final safety net, ensuring no account plan is ever left without a valid configuration. Admins manage this priority through a ranking interface, maintaining full visibility and control over how rules resolve when conditions overlap.

Context-aware configuration, applied automatically.

When a KAM creates a new account plan, they enter standard planning attributes: sales organization, distribution channel, division, customer, and planning period. Behind the scenes, SAP Revenue Growth Management matches these inputs against all active determination rules and applies the highest-ranked matching rule — and with it, the correct account plan type, and everything mapped to it. The KAM never selects a plan type manually. The right configuration is applied consistently, every time.

Configure once, scale everywhere — as sales areas expand, customers are added, or planning periods roll over, determination rules adapt dynamically, keeping every KAM in the right planning context without the manual maintenance that hard assignments demand.

 

4. Co-Innovation: Shape the Future of Business Configuration

Business Configuration in SAP Revenue Growth Management establishes a governed, flexible foundation for trade promotion planning — one that scales with your business and adapts to your market realities. And it is still evolving. We are actively developing the next generation of configuration capabilities, and we are looking for co-innovation partners to help us shape what comes next:

  • Configurable promotion overlapping rules: Extending the same determination logic that governs account plan types, a planned enhancement will allow admins to define how overlapping promotional conditions — whether by time period or by customer and product hierarchy level — interact and resolve, giving organizations precise control over multi-layered promotional scenarios.
  • Tactic configuration: Admins will be able to define custom promotion execution tactics — such as front display or temporary price reduction — and map them to promotion types, giving CPG companies structured visibility into how promotions are executed at the point of sale and enabling tactic-level analytics to inform future planning decisions.

If your organization is navigating the complexity of multi-market, multi-retailer trade promotion planning and wants to influence how SAP Revenue Growth Management evolves, we want to hear from you. Contact RGM_Engage@sap.com to join our co-innovation program and help build the next generation of configurable revenue growth management.

 

More Information

Product Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management 

Documentation on SAP Help Portal

Topic Page for SAP Revenue Growth Management

    • Introducing Calendar View in SAP Revenue Growth Management: A Visual Leap for Strategic Planning
    • Decoding Promotional Success: Embedded Analytics in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Unlocking Trade Spend Control Efficiency with Funds Management in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Consolidating Customer Planning: Unleash Growth with SAP Revenue Growth Management’s Account Plan
    • Elevate Your Promotion Strategy: Baseline Management with SAP Revenue Growth Management 
    • Driving Promotion Excellence: Streamlined Creation and Copying with SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Financial Execution & Claims for Trade Promotions with SAP Revenue Growth Management & SAP Cloud ERP
    • Master Your KPIs: How SAP Revenue Growth Management Boosts Data-Driven Growth 
    • Modernize Planning: Universal Hierarchy in SAP Revenue Growth Management for SAP Cloud ERP Customers
    • Promotion Creation Reimagined: AI‑Assisted Single & Bulk Workflows in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • One Plan, Multiple Measures: Driving Growth with Flexible Units in SAP Revenue Growth Management 
    • SAP Revenue Growth Management Integrated with SAP BDC: Seamless Data Integration & Governed Insights
    • Precision at Speed: AI‑Assisted Deal Sheet Generation in SAP Revenue Growth Management
    • Smarter Planning by Design: Introducing Business Configuration in SAP Revenue Growth Management

entry.title: HANA Storage Issue
entry.content:
I have cleaned up the *** pointer data via transaction BD22 by removing all records from *** . The table volume has decreased from *** million entries to *** million entries.

When checking large tables and disk consumption via transaction DB02, the table size did not shrink immediately (I noticed the table size was reduced the next day).
However, the database disk usage overview still shows *** GB occupied with no reduction.
Is it safe to purge all data from table *** entirely?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: HANA Storage Issue
entry.content:
1. As mentioned in the below note that you need use transaction BD22 and report RBDCPCLR there to delete data of table *** .

It looks you are performing this in the right way since mentioned about BD22, need your double confirm on this.

You could also use this note when you want to archive data for specific table, this can be simply by searching table name in this note.

- note
2388483 - How-To: Data Management for Technical Tables
-> ***

=>
513454 - REP: Achieving high performance when working with change pointers

2. After deleting data from all tables you want, you could then execute SQL command below to relaim size for log and data volume.

- SQL command
ALTER SYSTEM RECLAIM DATAVOLUME 120 DEFRAGMENT;
ALTER SYSTEM RECLAIM LOG;

- SQL Editor
3506374 - How-to: Execute SQL command for HANA DB

entry.title: Re: WFA on HANA error
entry.content:
Hi @abalasescu2 

Firstly, the error has nothing to do with this statement:

Creating function wfa_utc_to_tptz_nvarchar_ts (i_value nvarchar(5000) ) RETURNS e_res TIMESTAMP LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT SQL SECURITY INVOKER AS BEGIN DECLARE ts TIMESTAMP; DECLARE EXIT HANDLER FOR SQL_ERROR_CODE 303 e_res = null; ts = CAST( i_value AS SECONDDATE); e_res = (CASE WHEN cast(i_value as timestamp) <= '0001-01-02' THEN '0001-01-01 00:00:00' WHEN cast(i_value as timestamp) >= '9999-12-30' THEN '9999-12-31 23:59:59.999999' ELSE UTCTOLOCAL( cast(i_value as timestamp) , 'Europe/Warsaw','platform') END); END;

It just so happened there is an ERROR word in the above statement but that's not the actual error.  The actual error is this one in the log:

I've checked further and the problem is with Division dimension which is defined as manually maintained.  However, the configuration looks off.  For manually maintained dimension with SQL query to populate the description, the configuration UI should look like this:

But for Division dimension, it looks like this:

Most likely it was setup as Generated dimension and then changed to Manually Maintained and thus the issue occurs.  To fix it, just select Generated and then select Manually Maintained.  This will reset the UI and show the ID and Description column selection:

The SQL query itself is fine now.  Thank you.

entry.title: We are facing an issue while exporting data from the CJI3 report
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support Team,

We are facing an issue while exporting data from the CJI3 report.

Issue Description:

  • Transaction Code: CJI3
  • Selection Criteria:
    • Posting Date: 01.06.2026 to 30.06.2026
    • Projects: All Projects
  • The report executes successfully and the output screen is displayed.
  • However, when attempting to export/download the report output (Excel/Spreadsheet), the system throws an error and the data is not downloaded.

Expected Result: The report data should be exported successfully to Excel.

Actual Result: The system displays an error during export and the file is not downloaded.

entry.title: Validate G/L account in Account Assignment in Goods Issue with Movement Type 201
entry.content:
In the current process, users post Goods Issue using Movement Type 201, where a single cost center is used with multiple G/L accounts. Since the standard SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud system does not support configuring multiple G/L accounts for a single cost center, users are manually selecting the required G/L account, which increases the risk of incorrect postings. To avoid manual errors and improve process efficiency, an automation or validation is required to determine or validate the appropriate G/L account based on predefined business rules, ensuring that only the correct G/L account is posted against the respective cost center.

How can we achieve this ?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Validate G/L account in Account Assignment in Goods I...
entry.content:
Hi,

This can be achieve using Validation rules in Business Context Coding Block. Relevant Precondition and Validation fields are available like GoodsMovementType, Cost Center, Company Code, G/L Account, etc. Please refer to SAP Help Rules for Substitution/Validation > Coding Block Business Context for more information.

Regards,

An.

entry.title: Re: Bank Communication Capabilities in SAP S/4HANA Cloud...
entry.content:
Hi 6-Mar,

Short answer: Only SEPA Credit Transfer and SEPA Direct Debit are delivered as standard payment formats for Portugal in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. MT101, Confirming, Portuguese Tax (DUC), Social Security and Multibanco reference payments are not delivered as standard PT content — they can only be attempted via custom format mapping (self-service), subject to data-availability limits. Exchange rate reception is not a bank-communication message; it is handled through Market Rate Management.

The single most important source is the official list of SAP-delivered formats per country: for Portugal it contains only PT_CGI_XML_CT, PT_CGI_XML_CT_V9, PT_CGI_XML_DD, PT_CGI_XML_DD_V8 — nothing else. Reference: Country/Region-Specific Payment Format Mappings

Exchange Rates Reception — is it a supported bank communication service?

No. Exchange rates are not received as a bank message over the payment/statement channel. In Public Edition you load rates manually, by file upload, or automatically via SAP Market Rate Management (communication scenario SAP_COM_0203). Reference: How to Maintain Exchange Rates in SAP S/4HANA Cloud

 

SEPA Credit Transfer (SEPA CT) and SEPA Direct Debit (SEPA DD)— can it be implemented for Portugal?

Yes. Delivered standard as PT_CGI_XML_CT (pain.001.001.03) and PT_CGI_XML_CT_V9 (pain.001.001.09) for Credit Transfer and T_CGI_XML_DD (pain.008.001.02) and PT_CGI_XML_DD_V8 (pain.008.001.08) for Debit Transfer. Check the app Map Format Data for Payments.

MT101 Payment Orders — can it be implemented for Portugal?

As an outbound payment file format: No (not standard). SAP does not deliver a PT_MT101 format. MT101 is delivered for other countries (e.g. GB, IE, PL) but not Portugal, and MBC can transport MT101 as a message type. Reference:  MBC message types You can build a custom MT101 mapping in Map Format Data for Payments, but this is not out-of-the-box and must be validated and tested with your bank. Reference: Configuring Payment Medium Formats

If you mean matching the payment order (sent to the bank) on the bank statement for reconciliation: Yes, standard. The outgoing payment posts to a bank clearing (sub)account; when the bank statement (camt.053/MT940) is imported, the system automatically identifies, posts and clears the matching item (two-step reconciliation). Reference: Overview on Automatic Bank Statement Processing and Manage Processing Rules – For Bank Statements

  • In addition, the payment order status is returned by the bank as pain.002 and automatically updates the corresponding payment batch via MBC / Bank Communication Management. Reference: Importing Payment Statuses
  • Note: This reconciliation/status flow is independent of MT101 — it works the same with SEPA CT (pain.001), so it is available even though the PT_MT101 format is not delivered.

Portuguese Tax Payments (DUC), Social Security Payments, Multibanco References — can they be implemented?

No (not standard). None of these Portugal-domestic reference-payment formats are in the delivered PT content. These require a structured payment reference (DUC / Multibanco entity+reference / state payment reference) that is not part of the standard SEPA CT data — feasibility of a custom mapping depends on whether that data can be supplied as a custom reference field. Reather confirm with your bank whether these can be executed as a SEPA credit transfer carrying a structured reference, rather than a dedicated file format.

Confirming Payment Orders — can it be implemented for Portugal?

No (not standard). No "Confirming" (reverse-factoring / supplier-finance) payment format is delivered for Portugal or Spain in Public Edition; the delivered content is CGI/SEPA XML only. Any Confirming file would have to be a bank-specific custom format built via self-service and agreed with the bank — treat as project scope, not standard.

Important:

  • "Delivered standard" = SAP ships it ready to configure. "Custom mapping" = you build it yourself in Map Format Data for Payments under Localization as a Self-Service, with no guarantee the required domestic data fields are available. Reference: Localization as a Self-Service
  • Bank transport (file/EBICS/SWIFT/API, incl. MT101) runs through SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity; format generation and transport are two separate topics. Reference: SAP Note 3398869

Summary: For Portugal in Public Edition, plan on SEPA CT and SEPA DD as standard. Reconciling payment orders on the bank statement (EBS auto-matching + pain.002 status) is also standard — independent of MT101. Treat the MT101 file format, DUC/tax, Social Security, Multibanco references and Confirming as non-standard — each needs a custom format mapping (self-service) plus bank agreement, so first confirm the required data is available before committing scope. Handle exchange rates via Market Rate Management, not the bank channel.

Hope It helps.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI 10-second connect timeout (NiBufIConnect / WSAEWOULDBLOCK) — network's clean
entry.content:
Hello, the remark about 7.30 is more about security issues than solving your network layer issue. 

The GUI upgrade will not fix your lower layer issues. Since 8.10 will be released next week, it makes sense to plan your upgrade for this version rather than SAGPUI for Windows 8.00.
